The '''American goshawk''' ('''''Astur atricapillus''''') is a species of [[raptor (bird)|raptor]] in the family Accipitridae. It was first described by [[Alexander Wilson (ornithologist)|Alexander Wilson]] in 1812. The American goshawk was previously considered conspecific with the [[Eurasian goshawk]] but was assigned to a separate species in 2023 based on differences in morphology, vocalizations, and genetic divergence.<ref name=IOC/> It was formerly placed in the genus ''[[Accipiter]]''. It is mainly [[resident bird|resident]], but birds from colder regions [[bird migration|migrate]] south for the winter.<ref name=Ferguson-Lees>{{cite book |last1=Ferguson-Lees |first1=James |last2=Christie |first2=David A. |title=Raptors of the World |publisher=Houghton Mifflin Harcourt |year=2001 |isbn=978-0-618-12762-7 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=hlIztc05HTQC&q=northern+goshawk}}</ref> In North America, migratory goshawks are often seen migrating south along [[Ridge|mountain ridge]] tops at nearly any time of the fall depending on latitude.<ref name="Cornell" />

==Distribution==
In [[North America]], they are most broadly found in the [[Western United States]], including [[Alaska]], and [[western Canada]]. Their breeding range in the western [[contiguous United States]] largely consists of the wooded foothills of the [[Rocky Mountains]] and many other large mountain ranges from [[Washington (state)|Washington]] to [[southern California]] extending east to central [[Colorado]] and westernmost [[Texas]].<ref name="Cornell" /> Somewhat discontinuous breeding populations are found in southeastern [[Arizona]] and southwestern [[New Mexico]], thence also somewhat spottily into western Mexico down through [[Sonora]] and [[Chihuahua City|Chihuahua]] along the [[Sierra Madre Occidental]] as far as [[Jalisco]] and [[Guerrero]], their worldwide southern limit as a breeding species.<ref name=Ferguson-Lees/><ref>Monson, G. & Phillips, A. R. (1964). ''A check-list of the birds of Arizona''. Tucson: Univ. of Arizona Press.</ref><ref>Howell, S. N. G. & Webb, S. (1995). ''A guide to the birds of Mexico and northern Central America''. New York: Oxford University Press. {{ISBN|978-0198540120}}</ref>

The goshawk continues east through much of [[Canada]] as a native species, but is rarer in most of the [[Eastern United States]], especially the [[Midwestern United States|Midwest]], where they are not typically found outside the [[Great Lakes]] region, where a good-sized breeding population occurs in the northern parts of [[Minnesota]], [[Illinois]], [[Michigan]], and somewhat into [[Ohio]]; a very small population persists in the extreme northeast corner of [[North Dakota]]. They breed also in mountainous areas of [[New England]], [[New York (state)|New York]], central [[Pennsylvania]], and northwestern [[New Jersey]], sporadically down to extreme northwestern [[Maryland]] and northeastern [[West Virginia]].<ref name="Cornell" /> Vagrants have been reported in most of the parts of the [[United States]] in which they do not breed.<ref name="Ferguson-Lees" />

==Habitat==
[[File:Accipiter atricapillus Kaibab.jpg|thumb|left|Adult in the [[Kaibab Plateau]], [[Arizona]], in a [[pine]] tree that typifies the habitat used locally]]
American and Eurasian goshawks can be found in both [[deciduous]] and [[coniferous]] [[Temperate forest|forests]]. While the species might show strong regional preferences for certain trees, they seem to have no strong overall preferences nor even a preference between deciduous or coniferous trees despite claims to the contrary.<ref name="Brown"/><ref name=Ferguson-Lees/><ref name="Penteriani, V. 2002">{{cite journal |last1=Penteriani |first1=V |year=2002 |title=Goshawk nesting habitat in Europe and North America: a review |journal=Ornis Fennica |volume=79 |pages=149–163}}</ref><ref>{{cite web |url=https://www.gis.smumn.edu/GradProjects/LudwigT.pdf |title=Northern goshawk forest type preference in the Chippewa National Forest |last=Ludwig |first=T. W. |access-date=27 February 2017 |website=Saint Mary's University of Minnesota |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120901084255/http://www.gis.smumn.edu/GradProjects/LudwigT.pdf |archive-date=1 September 2012 }}</ref> More important than the type of trees are the composition of a given tree stand, which should be tall, [[Old-growth forest|old-growth]] with intermediate to heavy [[Canopy (biology)|canopy]] coverage (often more than 40%) and minimal density undergrowth, both of which are favorable for hunting conditions.<ref name="Cornell" /><ref>{{cite web |url=https://ir.library.oregonstate.edu/xmlui/bitstream/handle/1957/16275/AustinKarenK1994.pdf?sequence=1 |title= Habitat use and home range size of breeding northern goshawks in the southern Cascades |last=Austin |first=K. K. |date=1993 |format=PDF |type=Master's Thesis|website=Oregon State University |place=Corvallis, Oregon |access-date=27 February 2017}}</ref> Also, goshawks typically require proximity to [[Woodland edge|openings]] in which to execute additional hunting.<ref name="Kenward" /><ref>Erickson, M. G. (1987). ''Nest site habitat selection of the goshawk (Accipiter gentilis) in the Black Hills of South Dakota'' (Doctoral dissertation, MS thesis. University of South Dakota. Brookings, South Dakota).</ref> 

Access to [[Wetland|waterways]] and [[riparian zone]]s of any kind is not uncommon in goshawk home ranges but seems to not be a requirement.<ref name= Bull>{{cite journal | last1 = Bull | first1 = E. L. | last2 = Hohmann | first2 = J. E. | year = 1994 | title = Breeding biology of northern goshawks in northeastern Oregon | journal = Studies in Avian Biology | volume = 16 | pages = 103–105 }}</ref> Narrow tree-lined riparian zones in otherwise relatively open habitats can provide suitable wintering habitat in the absence of more extensive woodlands.<ref name= Squires>{{cite journal | last1 = Squires | first1 = J. R. Ruggiero | year = 1995 | title = Winter movements of adult northern goshawks that nested in southcentral Wyoming | journal = J. Raptor Res. | volume = 29 | pages = 5–9 }}</ref> The American goshawk can be found at almost any altitude, but recently is typically found at [[Montane forest|high elevations]] due to a paucity of extensive forests remaining in lowlands across much of its range. Altitudinally, goshawks may live anywhere up to a given mountain range's [[tree line]], which is usually {{convert|3000|m|ft|abbr=on}} in elevation or less.<ref name=Ferguson-Lees/> In winter months, the northernmost or high mountain populations move down to warmer forests with lower elevations, often continuing to avoid detection except while migrating. A majority of goshawks around the world remain sedentary throughout the year.<ref name="Cornell" /><ref name="Johnsgard" />

==Description==
===Similar species===
[[File:Coopers-Hawk-on-a-Fence.jpg|thumb|upright|Large juvenile [[Cooper's hawk]]s such as this one are at times mistaken for a juvenile goshawk]]
The juvenile plumage of the species may cause some confusion, especially with other juvenile ''Astur'' species. Unlike other northern ''Astur'', the adult American and Eurasian goshawk never has a rusty color to its underside barring.<ref name="Kenward" /> Wing beats of American goshawks are deeper, more deliberate, and on average slower than those of the two other North American ''Asturs''.<ref name= Cramp>Cramp, S. and K. E. L. Simmons. (1980). ''Handbook of the birds of Europe and the Middle East and North Africa: the birds of the western Palearctic, Vol. 2. Hawks to bustards''. Oxford: Oxford Univ. Press. {{ISBN|9780198575054}}</ref><ref>Kaufman, K. (1990). ''A Field Guide to advanced birding: birding challenges and how to approach them''. Boston, MA: Houghton Mifflin Co. {{ISBN|9780395535172}}</ref>

American goshawks are sometimes mistaken for species even outside of the genus ''Astur'' especially as juveniles of each respective species. In [[North America]], four species of [[Buteoninae|buteonine hawk]] (all four of which are smaller than goshawks to a certain degree) may be confused with them on occasion despite the differing proportions of these hawks, which all have longer wings and shorter tails relative to their size. A species so similar it is sometimes nicknamed the "Mexican goshawk", [[gray hawk]] (''Buteo plagiatus'') juveniles (overlapping with true goshawks in the southwest United States into Mexico) have contrasting face pattern with bold dusky eye-stripes, dark eyes, barred thighs and a bold white "U" on the uppertail coverts. The [[roadside hawk]] (''Rupornis magnirostris'') (rarely in same range in Mexico) is noticeably smaller with paddle-shaped wings, barred lower breast and a buff "U" on undertail coverts in young birds. Somewhat less likely to confuse despite their broader extent of overlap are the [[red-shouldered hawk]] (''Buteo lineatus'') which have a narrow white-barred, dark-looking tail, bold white crescents on their primaries and dark wing edges and the [[broad-winged hawk]] (''Buteo playpterus'') which also has dark wing edges and a differing tapered wing shape. Even wintering [[gyrfalcon]] (''Falco rusticolus'') juveniles have been mistaken for goshawks and vice versa on occasion, especially when observed distantly perched. However, the bulkier, broader headed yet relatively shorter tailed falcon still has many tell-tale falcon characteristics like pointed, longer wings, a brown malar stripe as well as its more extensive barring both above and below.<ref name=Ferguson-Lees/>

[[File:The Oölogist for the student of birds, their nests and eggs (1911) (14562268417).jpg|thumb|upright|Two juveniles from [[Pennsylvania]] after they've become "branchers", or have left the nest but are not yet flying competently]]
Juveniles are sometimes confused with the smaller [[Cooper's hawk]] (''Astur cooperii''), especially juvenile Cooper's hawks. Unlike in [[Europe]] with sparrowhawks, Cooper's hawks can have a largish appearance and juveniles may be regularly mistaken for the usually less locally numerous goshawk. However, the juvenile goshawk displays a heavier, vertical streaking pattern on chest and abdomen, with the juvenile Cooper's hawk streaking frequently (but not always) in a "teardrop" pattern wherein the streaking appears to taper at the top, as opposed to the more even streaking of the goshawk. The goshawk sometimes seems to have a shorter tail relative to its much broader body. Although there appears to be a size overlap between small male goshawks and large female Cooper's hawks, [[morphometric]] measurements (wing and tail length) of both species demonstrate no such overlap, although weight overlap can rarely occur due to variation in seasonal condition and food intake at time of weighing.<ref name= Mueller>{{cite journal|last1=Mueller |first1=Helmut C. |last2=Berger |first2=Daniel D. |last3=Allez |first3=George |title=The Identification of North American Accipiters |journal= American Birds |volume=33 |number=3 |pages=236–240 |date=May 1979 |url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/nab/v033n03/p00236-p00240.pdf |issn=0004-7686 |access-date= 28 February 2017}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last1=Smith |first1=Jeff P. |last2=Hoffman |first2=Stephen W. |last3=Gessaman |first3= James A. |date=Spring 1990 |title=Regional Size Differences among Fall-Migrant Accipiters in North America |journal=Journal of Field Ornithology |volume=61 |number=2 |pages=192–200 |url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/jfo/v061n02/p0192-p0200.pdf |issn=0273-8570 |access-date=11 March 2017}}.</ref>

== Taxonomy ==
The genus ''Astur'' contains nine living species. This group of agile, smallish, forest-dwelling hawks has been in existence for possibly tens of millions of years, probably as an adaptation to the explosive numbers of small birds that began to occupy the world's forest in the last few eras. The [[harrier (bird)|harrier]]s are the only group of extant diurnal raptors that seem to bear remotely close relation to this genus, whereas [[Buteoninae|buteonines]], [[Milvinae|Old World kites]], [[sea eagle]]s and [[Melierax|chanting-goshawk]]s are much more distantly related and all other modern accipitrids are not directly related.<ref name=Ferguson-Lees/><ref name="Kenward" />

Within the genus ''Astur'', the American goshawk seems to belong to a [[Species complex|superspecies]] with other larger goshawks from different portions of the world.<ref name="Brown"/><ref>Kleinschmidt, O. (1923). ''Berajah, Zoografia infinita. Die Realgattung Habicht, Falco columbarius (KL)''. Gebauer-Schwetschke, Halle, Germany.</ref> [[Eurasian goshawk]], found in [[Eurasia]], was previously considered conspecific with American goshawk, formed the species complex "northern goshawk". [[Meyer's goshawk]], found in the [[South Pacific Ocean|South Pacific]], has been posited as the most likely to be the closest related living cousin to the northern goshawk, the somewhat puzzling gap in their respective ranges explained by other [[Palearctic]] raptors such as [[Bonelli's eagle]]s (''Aquila fasciata'') and [[short-toed eagle]]s (''Circaetus gallicus'') that have extant isolated tropical island populations and were probably part of the same southwest Pacific radiation that led to the Meyer's goshawk.<ref name= Wattel>{{cite journal |last=Wattel |first=Jan |title=Geographical differentiation in the genus Accipiter |journal= Publications of the Nuttall Ornithological Club |year=1973 |number=13 |location=Cambridge, Mass. |isbn=978-1-877973-23-9}}.</ref> A presumably older radiation of this group may have occurred in Africa, where it led to both the [[Henst's goshawk]] of [[Madagascar]] and the [[black sparrowhawk]] (''Astur melanoleucus'') of the mainland. While the Henst's goshawk quite resembles the northern goshawks, the black sparrowhawk is superficially described as a "sparrowhawk" due to its relatively much longer and finer legs than those of typical goshawks but overall its size and plumage (especially that of juveniles) is much more goshawk than sparrowhawk-like.<ref name="Kenward" /><ref name="Wattel" />

Genetic studies have indicated that the [[Cooper's hawk]] of North America is also fairly closely related to the northern goshawk, having been present in North America before either of the two North American species of ''Astur''. However, the much smaller [[sharp-shinned hawk]], which has [[Covergent evolution|similar plumage]] to the Cooper's hawk and seems to be most closely related to the [[Eurasian sparrowhawk]], appears to have occupied North America the latest of the three North American species of [[Accipitrinae]], despite having the broadest current distribution of any of the American members of Accipitrinae (extending down through much of [[South America]]).<ref name="Cornell" /><ref name="Kenward" />

===Subspecies===
[[File:Goshawkmale66.jpg|thumb|Typical adult from the American subspecies (''A. a. atricapillus'') showing its strong supercilium, red eyes, black head, and blue-gray back]]
*{{anchor|Astur atricapillus atricapillus}}''A. a. atricapillus'' ([[Alexander Wilson (ornithologist)|Wilson]], 1812) – This subspecies occupies a majority of the species' range in North America, excluding some islands of the [[Pacific Northwest]] and the southern part of the [[American Southwest]]. American goshawks are generally slightly smaller on average than most Eurasian ones, although there are regional differences in size that confirm mildly to [[Bergmann's rule]] within this race. Furthermore, sexual dimorphism in size is notably less pronounced in American goshawks than in most Eurasian races. Overall, the wing chord is {{convert|308|to|337|mm|in|abbr=on}} in males and {{convert|324|to|359|mm|in|abbr=on}} in females.<ref name=Ferguson-Lees/> Size within ''atricapillus'' based on body mass seems to be highest in interior [[Alaska]], followed by the [[Great Lakes]], is intermediate in the northwest United States from eastern [[Washington (state)|Washington]] to the [[Dakotas]] as well as in southeast Alaska thence decreasing mildly along the Pacific in [[Oregon]] and California and smallest of all within the race in the [[Great Basin]] and [[Colorado Plateau]] states (i.e. [[Nevada]], [[Utah]] and northern and central [[Arizona]]). Conspicuously, wing size did not correspond to variations in body mass and more southerly goshawks were frequently longer winged than the more massive northerly ones.<ref name= Palmer>{{cite book|last=Palmer |first=Ralph S. |title=Handbook of North American Birds, Vol. 4: Diurnal Raptors. Pt. 1 |location=New Haven, CT |publisher=Yale University Press |date=1988 |isbn=978-0-300-04059-3}}</ref><ref name= Smith>Smith, J. P., Hoffman, S. W., & Gessaman, J. A. (1990). ''Regional Size Differences among Fall-Migrant Accipiters in North America''. Journal of Field Ornithology, 192–200.</ref><ref>Whaley, W. H. & White, C.M. (1994). ''Trends in geographic variation of Cooper's hawk and northern goshawk in North America: a multivariate analysis''. Proc. West. Found. Vertebr. Zool. no. 5:161-209.</ref> Male ''atricapillus'' goshawk have been found to weigh from {{convert|655|to|1200|g|lb|abbr=on}} and females from {{convert|758|to|1562|g|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name=Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= "CRC">Dunning Jr., John B. (ed.) (2008). ''CRC Handbook of Avian Body Masses, 2nd Edition''. CRC Press. {{ISBN|978-1-4200-6444-5}}.</ref><ref name= Palmer/><ref>Henny, C. J., Olson. R. A. & Fleming, T. L. (1985). ''Breeding chronology, molt, and measurements of accipiter hawks in northeastern Oregon''. J. Field Ornithol. no. 56: 97–112.</ref> The lightest reported mean weights were from goshawks in northern and central Arizona, weighing a mean of {{convert|680|g|lb|abbr=on}} in males and {{convert|935|g|lb|abbr=on}} while the highest were from a small sample of Alaskan goshawks which weighed some {{convert|905|g|lb|abbr=on}} in males and {{convert|1190|g|lb|abbr=on}} in females.<ref name="Brown">{{cite book |first1=Leslie |last1=Brown |first2=Dean |last2=Amadon |title=Eagles, Hawks and Falcons of the World |publisher=Wellfleet |year=1986 |isbn=978-1-55521-472-2 }}</ref><ref>Mavrogordato, J. (1973). ''A Hawk for the Bush: A Treatise on the Training of the Sparrow-hawk and Other Short-winged Hawks''. Spearman.</ref><ref>Mueller, H. C., Berger, D. D. & Allez, G. (1976). ''Age and sex variation in the size of goshawks''. Bird-Banding no. 47 (4):310–318.</ref> Almost identical mean weights for goshawks as in [[Alaska]] were recorded for goshawks from [[Alberta]] as well.<ref name= Palmer/> This race is typically a blue-gray color above with a boldly contrasting black head and broad white supercilia. American goshawks are often grayish below with fine gray waving barring and, compared to most Eurasian goshawks, rather apparent black shaft streaks which in combination create a vermiculated effect that is all-together messier looking than in most Eurasian birds. From a distance, ''atricapillus'' can easily appear solidly all-gray from the front.<ref name=Ferguson-Lees/> Due to this, the adult goshawk in America is sometimes called the "gray ghost", a name also somewhat more commonly used for adult male [[hen harrier]]s.<ref name=Rashid>Rashid, S. (2015). ''Northern Goshawk, the Grey Ghost: Habits, Habitats and Rehabilitation''. Schiffer Publishing Ltd.</ref> Birds from mainland Alaska tend to be paler overall with more pale flecking than other American goshawks.<ref name="Kenward" />
*{{anchor|Astur atricapillus laingi}}''A. a. laingi'' [[Percy A. Taverner|Tavernier]], 1940 – This insular race is found on [[Haida Gwaii]] and [[Vancouver Island]]. This subspecies is slightly smaller than the goshawks found on the mainland and is linearly the smallest race on average in North America. The wing chord of males can range from {{convert|312|to|325|mm|in|abbr=on}} and that of females is {{convert|332|to|360|mm|in|abbr=on}} and is on average nearly 5% smaller than those sampled goshawks from the nearby mainland.<ref name=Ferguson-Lees/><ref name=Johnson1989>{{cite journal |last=Johnson |first=Donald R. |title=Body size of Northern Goshawks on coastal islands of British Columbia |url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/wilson/v101n04/p0637-p0639.pdf |journal=Wilson Bulletin |volume=101 |issue=4 |date= December 1989 |pages=637–639 |issn=1559-4491 |access-date=1 March 2017}}</ref> These goshawks are characteristically darker than mainland goshawks with the black of the crown extending to the interscapulars. The underside is a sootier gray overall.<ref name=Johnson1989/><ref>{{cite journal |last1= Taverner |first1= P. A. |year=1940 |title= Variation in the American goshawk |journal= The Condor |volume= 42 |issue= 3|pages= 157–160 |doi=10.2307/1364206|jstor= 1364206 }}</ref><ref>Iverson, G. C., Hayward, G. D., Titus, K. DeGayner, E., Lowell, R. E., Crocker-Bedford, D. C., Schempf, P. F. & Lindell, J. F. (1996). ''Conservation assessment for the northern goshawk in southeast Alaska''. U.S. Dept., Agric., For. Serv. Publ.</ref>
*{{anchor|Astur atricapillus apache}}''A. a. apache'' ([[Adriaan Joseph van Rossem|van Rossem]], 1938) – The range of this subspecies extends from southern Arizona and [[New Mexico]] down throughout the species' range in [[Mexico]]. This subspecies has the longest median wing size of any race, running contrary to Bergmann's rule that northern birds should outsize southern ones in widely distributed temperate species. In males the [[Wing chord (biology)|wing chord]] ranges from {{convert|344|to|354|mm|in|abbr=on}} while in females it ranges from {{convert|365|to|390|mm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref name=Ferguson-Lees/> However, in terms of body mass, it is only slightly heavier than the goshawks found discontinuously somewhat to the north in the [[Great Basin]] and the [[Colorado Plateau]] and lighter than the heaviest known American goshawks from Alaska, Alberta and [[Wisconsin]] despite exceeding the goshawks from these areas in wing size. The weight of 49 males ranged from {{convert|631|to|744|g|lb|abbr=on}}, averaging {{convert|704|g|lb|abbr=on}}, while that of 88 females from two studies ranged from {{convert|845|to|1265|g|lb|abbr=on}}, averaging {{convert|1006|g|lb|abbr=on}}.<ref name= "CRC"/><ref>Ingraldi, M.F. (2005). ''A skewed sex ratio in Northern Goshawks: is it a sign of a stressed population?'' Journal of Raptor Research 39.3 (2005): 247–252.</ref> Aside from its overall larger size, ''apache'' reportedly averages larger in foot size than most other American goshawks. Birds of this race tend to be darker than other American goshawks aside from the ''laingi'' type birds. Due to its shortage of distinct features beyond proportions, this is considered one of the more weakly separated among current separate subspecies, with some authors considering it merely a clinal variation of ''atricapillus''. Even the greater wing size in southern birds follows a trend for the wing chord to increase in size in the south on the contrary to body mass.<ref name=Ferguson-Lees/><ref name="Cornell" /><ref name= Smith/>

==Behavior==
===Migration===
Migratory goshawks in North America may move down to [[Baja California]], [[Sinaloa]] and into most of [[west Texas]], but generally in non-irruptive years, goshawks winter no further south than [[Nebraska]], [[Iowa]], [[Illinois]], [[Indiana]], eastern [[Tennessee]] and western [[North Carolina]].<ref name=Ferguson-Lees/><ref name= Squires/><ref>Keane, J. J. & Morrison, M.L. (1994). "Northern Goshawk ecology: effects of scale and levels of biological organization". ''Stud. Avian Biol.'' no. 16:3-11.</ref> Some periodic eruptions to nearly as far as the [[Gulf of Mexico]] have been recorded at no fewer than 10 years apart. In one case, a female that was banded in [[Wisconsin]] was recovered {{convert|1860|km|mi|abbr=on}} in [[Louisiana]], a first ever record of the species in that state.<ref>Evans, D. L. & Sindelar, C. R. (1974). "First record of the goshawk for Louisiana-a collected, banded bird"". ''Bird-Banding'', no. 45: 270.</ref>

Prey availability may primarily dictate the proportion of goshawk populations that migrate and the selection of wintering areas, followed by the presence of snow which may aid prey capture in the short-term but in the long-term is likely to cause higher goshawk mortality.<ref name="Kenward" /><ref name="Marcström">Marcström, V., & Kenward, R. (1981). ''Movements of wintering goshawks in Sweden''. Swedish Sportsmen's Association.</ref><ref name= Doyle>Doyle, F. I. & Smith, J. M. N. (1994). "Population responses of northern goshawks to the 10-year cycle in numbers of snowshoe hares". ''Stud. Avian Biol.'' no. 16:122–129.</ref> Showing the high variability of migratory movements, in one study of winter movements of adult female goshawks that bred in high-elevation forests of [[Utah]], about 36% migrated {{convert|100|to|613|km|mi|abbr=on}} to the general south, 22% migrated farther than that distance, 8.3% migrated less far, 2.7% went north instead of south and 31% stayed throughout winter on their breeding territory.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Sonsthagen | first1 = S. A. | last2 = Rodriguez | first2 = R. | last3 = White | first3 = C. M. | year = 2006 | title = Satellite telemetry of Northern Goshawks breeding in Utah-I. Annual movements | journal = Studies in Avian Biology | volume = 31 | page = 239 }}</ref> Irruptive movements seem to occur for northern populations, i.e. those of the [[Taiga|boreal forests]] in [[North America]], [[Scandinavia]], and possibly [[Siberia]], with more equal sex ratio of movement and a strong southward tendency of movements in years where prey such as [[hare]]s and [[grouse]] crash.<ref name="Kenward" /> Male young goshawks tend to disperse farther than females, which is unusual in birds, including raptors.<ref name="Marcström"/><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Haukioja |first1=E. |last2=Haukioja |first2=M. |year=1970 |title=''Mortality rates of Finnish and Swedish goshawks (Accipiter gentilis)'' |journal=Finnish Game Research |volume=31 |pages=13–20}}</ref> It has been speculated that larger female juveniles displace male juveniles, forcing them to disperse farther, to the incidental benefit of the species' genetic diversity. In [[Cedar Grove, Wisconsin]], there were more than twice as many juvenile males than females recorded migrating.<ref>Mueller, H. C., D. D. Berger and G. Allez. 1977. "The periodic invasions of goshawks". ''Auk'' no. 94:652-663.</ref> At the hawk watch at [[Cape May Point State Park]] in [[New Jersey]], few adult males and no adult females have been recorded in fall migration apart from irruptive years, indicating that migration is more important to juveniles.<ref>Sibley, D. A. 1997. ''Birds of Cape May'', 2nd ed. Cape May Point: New Jersey Audubon Soc.</ref> More juveniles were recorded migrating than adults in several years of study from Sweden.<ref name="Marcström"/> In northern ''Asturs'' including the goshawk, there seems to be multiple peaks in numbers of migrants, an observation that suggests partial segregation by age and sex.<ref>Hoffman, S. W., W. R. DeRagon and J. C. Bednarz. (1992). ''Patterns and recent trends in counts of migrant hawks in western North America, 1977-1991''. Unpubl. report.</ref>

==Dietary biology==

===Hunting behavior===
In [[North America]], goshawks are generally rather more likely than those from Eurasia to hunt within the confines of mature forest, excluding areas where prey numbers are larger outside of the forest, such as where scrub-dwelling [[cottontail]]s are profuse.<ref name= Bright-Smith>{{cite journal |last1= Bright-Smith |first1= D. J. |last2= Mannan |first2= R. W. |year=1994 |title= Habitat use by breeding male northern goshawks in northern Arizona |journal= Stud. Avian Biol. |volume= 16 |pages= 58–65 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1= Beier |first1= P. |last2= Drennan |first2= J. E. |year=1997 |title= Forest structure and prey abundance in foraging areas of northern goshawks |journal= Ecological Applications |volume= 7 |issue= 2|pages= 564–571 |doi=10.2307/2269521|jstor= 2269521 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1= Underwood |first1= J. |last2= White |first2= C. M. |last3= Rodriguez |first3= R. |year=2006 |title= Winter movement and habitat use of northern goshawks breeding in Utah |journal= Studies in Avian Biology |volume= 31 |page= 228 }}</ref>

===Prey spectrum===

Northern goshawks are usually opportunistic predators, as are most [[Bird of prey|birds of prey]]. The most important prey species are small to medium-sized [[mammal]]s and medium to large-sized birds found in forest, edge and scrub habitats.<ref name="Reynolds" />

However, a few prey families dominate the diet in most parts of the range, namely [[corvid]]s, [[pigeon]]s, [[grouse]], [[Phasianidae|pheasant]]s, [[thrush (bird)|thrush]]es and [[woodpecker]]s (in roughly descending order of importance) among birds and [[squirrel]]s (mainly [[tree squirrel]]s but also [[ground squirrel]]s especially in North America) and [[Leporidae|rabbits and hare]]s among mammals.<ref name="Kenward" /><ref name="Cornell" /><ref name=Smithers2005>{{cite journal |last1=Smithers |first1=B. L. |last2=Boal |first2=C. W. |last3=Andersen |first3=D. E. |url=http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/jrr/v039n03/p00264-p00273.pdf |title=Northern Goshawk diet in Minnesota: An analysis using video recording systems |journal=Journal of Raptor Research |year=2005 |volume=39 |issue=3 |pages=264–273}}</ref><ref name=animaldiversity>{{cite web |url=http://animaldiversity.ummz.umich.edu/site/accounts/information/Accipiter_gentilis.html |title=Accipiter gentilis – northern goshawk |website=Animal Diversity Web |publisher=University of Michigan}}</ref><ref name=Lewis2006>{{cite journal |last1=Lewis |first1=Stephen B. |last2=Titus |first2=Kimberly |last3=Fuller |first3=Mark R. |url=http://fresc.usgs.gov/products%5Cpapers%5C1547_Fuller.pdf |title=Northern Goshawk Diet During the Nesting Season in Southeast Alaska |doi=10.2193/0022-541X(2006)70[1151:NGDDTN]2.0.CO;2 |year=2006 |journal=Journal of Wildlife Management |volume=70 |issue=4 |pages=1151–1160|s2cid=59493648 }}</ref>

Birds constitute 47.8% in 33 studies and mammals account for a nearly equal portion of the diet and in some areas rather dominate the food spectrum.<ref name= Kennedy>Kennedy, P. L. (2003). ''Northern goshawk (Accipiter gentilis atricapillus): A technical conservation assessment''. USFS, Rocky Mountain Region, Species Conservation Project. Fort Collins, Colorado.</ref><ref name= Bosakowski>Bosakowski, T. (1999). ''The Northern Goshawk: Ecology, Behavior and Management in North America''. Hancock House Pub Limited.</ref><ref>Jones, S. (1979). ''The accipiters: goshawk, Cooper's hawk, sharp-shinned hawk (No. 17)''. US Dept. of the Interior, Bureau of Land Management.</ref> There is some difference in size and type between the prey caught by males and larger females. Prey selection between sexes is more disparate in the more highly dimorphic races from Eurasia than those from North America. The average prey caught by each sex in [[Arizona]] was {{convert|281.5|g|oz|abbr=on}} and {{convert|380.4|g|oz|abbr=on}}, respectively, or around a 26% difference.<ref>Boal, C. W., & Mannan, R. W. (1996). ''Prey sizes of male and female northern goshawks''. The Southwestern Naturalist, 355–358.</ref>

===Corvids===
Overall, one prey family that is known to be taken in nearly every part of the goshawk's range is the corvids, although they do not necessarily dominate the diet in all areas. Some 24 species have been reported in the diet of Eurasian and American goshawks. The second most commonly reported prey species in breeding season dietary studies from North America is the {{convert|128|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[Steller's jay]] (''Cyanocitta stelleri''). These species were recorded in studies from northwestern [[Oregon]] and the [[Kaibab Plateau]] of [[Arizona]] (where the Steller's made up 37% by number) as the main prey species by number. The conspicuously loud vocalizations, somewhat sluggish flight (when hunting adult or post-fledging individuals) and moderate size of these jays make them ideal for prey-gathering male goshawks.<ref name= "CRC"/><ref name="Reynolds" /><ref name= Zawadzka>{{cite journal | last1 = Zawadzka | first1 = D. | last2 = Zawadzki | first2 = J. | year = 1998 | title = The Goshawk Accipiter gentilis in Wigry National Park (NE Poland)-numbers, breeding results, diet composition and prey selection | journal = Acta Ornithologica | volume = 33 | issue = 3–4| pages = 181–190 }}</ref><ref name= Penteriani>{{cite journal |last1= Penteriani |first1= V |year=1997 |title= Long-term study of a Goshawk breeding population on a Mediterranean mountain (Abruzzi Apennines, Central, Italy): density, breeding performance and diet |journal= Journal of Raptor Research |volume= 31 |pages= 308–312 }}</ref><ref name= Boal>{{cite journal | last1 = Boal | first1 = C. W. | last2 = Mannan | first2 = R. W. | year = 1994 | title = Northern goshawk diets in ponderosa pine forests on the Kaibab Plateau | journal = Studies in Avian Biology | volume = 16 | pages = 97–102 }}</ref> In the following areas ''[[Corvus]]'' species were the leading prey by number: the {{convert|457|g|lb|abbr=on}} [[American crow]] (''Corvus brachyrhynchos'') in [[New York (state)|New York]] and [[Pennsylvania]] (44.8% by number).<ref name= "CRC"/><ref name= Karyakin>Karyakin, I. (2009). ''Goshawk in the Urals and adjacent territories''. Raptor Conservation, A (4).</ref><ref name= Araujo>{{cite journal |last1=Araujo |first1=J |year=1974 |title=Falconiformes del Guadarrama suroccidental |journal=Ardeola |volume=19 |issue=2 |pages=257–278}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Zuban |first1=I.A. |year=2012 |title=Goshawk Accipiter gentilis in Zhambyl district of North Kazakhstan |journal=Russian Ornithological Journal |volume=749 |issue=21 |pages=885–890}}</ref><ref name= Meng>{{cite journal |last1=Meng |first1=H |year=1959 |title=Food habits of nesting Cooper's Hawks and Goshawks in New York and Pennsylvania |journal=The Wilson Bulletin |volume=71 |issue=2 |pages=169–174}}</ref> Despite evidence that northern goshawks avoid nesting near [[common raven]]s (''Corvus corax''), the largest widespread corvid (about the same size as a goshawk at {{convert|1040|g|lb|abbr=on}}) and a formidable opponent even one-on-one, they are even known to prey on ravens seldom.<ref name="Kenward" /><ref name= "CRC"/><ref name= Zawadzka/><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Ellenberg |first1=H. |last2=Dreifke |first2=R. |year=1993 |title="Abrition"– Der Kolrabe als Schutzchild vor dem Habicht |journal=Corax |volume=15 |pages=2–10}}</ref> Corvids taken have ranged in size from the {{convert|72|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[Canada jay]] (''Perisoreus canadensis'') to the raven.<ref name= "CRC"/><ref name= Watson>{{cite journal |last1= Watson |first1= J. W. |last2= Hays |first2= D. W. |last3= Finn |first3= S. P. |last4= Meehan-Martin |first4= P. |year=1998 |title= Prey of breeding northern goshawks in Washington |journal= Journal of Raptor Research |volume= 32 |issue= 4|pages= 297–305 }}</ref>

===Pigeons and doves===
Most of American goshawks take pigeons less commonly than Eurasian goshawks.<ref name="Kenward" /><ref>{{cite journal |last1= Takagi |first1= M. |last2= Ueta |first2= M. |last3= Ikeda |first3= S. |year=1995 |title= Accipiters prey on nestling birds in Japan |journal= Journal of Raptor Research |volume= 29 |pages= 267–268 }}</ref><ref name="Bosakowski2" /> One exception is in [[Connecticut]] where the [[mourning dove]] (''Zenaida macroura''), the smallest known pigeon or dove the goshawk has hunted at {{convert|119|g|oz|abbr=on}}, was the second most numerous prey species.<ref name= "CRC"/><ref name="Becker" /> 

===Gamebirds===

The American goshawk is in some parts of its range considered a specialized predator of [[Galliformes|gamebirds]], particularly [[grouse]]. All told 33 species of this order have turned up in American and [[Eurasian goshawk]]s' diet, including most of the species either native to or introduced in North America and Europe. Numerically, only in the well-studied [[taiga]] habitats of [[Canada]] and [[Alaska]] and some areas of the [[eastern United States]] do grouse typically take a dominant position. Elsewhere in the range, gamebirds are often secondary in number but often remain one of the most important contributors of prey biomass to nests. With their general ground-dwelling habits, gamebirds tend to be fairly easy for goshawks to overtake if they remain unseen and, if made aware of the goshawk, the prey chooses to run rather than fly. If frightened too soon, gamebirds may take flight and may be chased for some time, although the capture rates are reduced considerably when this occurs. Pre-fledgling chicks of gamebirds are particularly vulnerable due to the fact that they can only run when being pursued.<ref name="Brown"/><ref name="Kenward" /> There are impressive feats of attacks on other particularly large gamebirds have been reported, in at least one case, successful predation on an estimated {{convert|3900|g|lb|abbr=on}} adult-sized young [[wild turkey]] (''Meleagris gallopavo'') hen by an immature female goshawk weighing approximately {{convert|1050|g|lb|abbr=on}}), although taking adults of much larger-bodied prey like this is considered generally rare, the young chicks and poults of such prey species are likely much more often taken.<ref>{{cite journal |last1= Golet |first1= G. H. |last2= Golet |first2= H. T. |last3= Colton |first3= A. M. |year=2003 |title= Immature Northern Goshawk captures, kills, and feeds on adult-sized wild turkey |journal= Journal of Raptor Research |volume= 37 |issue= 4|pages= 337–340 }}</ref>

Despite reports that grouse are less significant as prey to American goshawks, the {{convert|560|g|lb|abbr=on}} [[ruffed grouse]] (''Bonasa umbellus'') is one of the most important prey species in North America (fourth most reported prey species in 22 studies), having been the leading prey species for goshawks in studies from [[New York (state)|New York]], [[New Jersey]] and [[Connecticut]] (from 12 to 25% of prey selected) and reported as taken in high numbers elsewhere in several parts of their mutual range.<ref name="Bosakowski2" /><ref name="Becker" /><ref name="Grzybowski, J. A. 1976" /><ref name= Graham>Graham, R. T., de Volo, S. B., & Reynolds, R. T. (2015). ''Northern goshawk and its prey in the Black Hills: Habitat assessment''. Gen. Tech. Rep. RMRS-GTR-339. Fort Collins, CO: US, Department of Agriculture, Forest Service, Rocky Mountain Research Station.</ref> The {{convert|1056|g|lb|abbr=on}} [[sooty grouse]] (''Dendragapus fuliginosus'') was reported as the leading prey species in southern [[Alaska]] (28.4% by number).<ref name= "CRC"/><ref name=Lewis2006>{{cite journal |last1=Lewis |first1=Stephen B. |last2=Titus |first2=Kimberly |last3=Fuller |first3=Mark R. |url=http://fresc.usgs.gov/products%5Cpapers%5C1547_Fuller.pdf |title=Northern Goshawk Diet During the Nesting Season in Southeast Alaska |doi=10.2193/0022-541X(2006)70[1151:NGDDTN]2.0.CO;2 |year=2006 |journal=Journal of Wildlife Management |volume=70 |issue=4 |pages=1151–1160|s2cid=59493648 }}</ref><ref>The Shipley Group (2009). ''Goshawk Survey; Soule River Watershed, Southeast Alaska''. Alaska Power & Telephone Survey.</ref> In the boreal forests of [[Alberta]], grouse are fairly important prey especially in winter.<ref name= Schaffer>Schaffer, W. W. 1998. ''Northern goshawk (Accipiter gentilis) habitat characterization in central Alberta''. Thesis, University of Alberta, Edmonton, Alberta.</ref>

===Squirrels===
[[File:Martes americana Yellowstone 1.jpg|thumb|upright|Prey selection frequently overlaps between goshawks and [[American marten]]s, seldom will both species prey on the other]]
In [[North America]], tree squirrels are even more significant as prey, particularly the modestly-sized [[pine squirrel]]s which are the single most important prey type for American goshawks overall. Particularly the {{convert|240|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[American red squirrel]] (''Tamiasciurus hudsonicus'') is significant, being the primary prey in studies from [[Minnesota]], [[South Dakota]], [[Wyoming]] and [[Montana]] (in each comprising more than 30% of the diet and present in more than half of known pellets) but also reported everywhere in their foods from the [[eastern United States]] to [[Alaska]] and [[Arizona]]. Much like the [[American marten]] (''Martes americana''), the American distribution of goshawks is largely concurrent with that of American red squirrels, indicating the particular significance of it as a dietary staple.<ref name=Smithers2005>{{cite journal |last1=Smithers |first1=B. ===Hares and rabbits===
Mammals are more important in their diet than in [[Eurasian goshawk]]'s, more lagomorphs are taken. In [[Oregon]], [[snowshoe hare]]s (''Lepus americanus'') are the largest contributor of biomass to goshawks foods (making up to 36.6% of the prey by weight), in eastern Oregon at least 60% of hares taken were adults weighing on average {{convert|1500|g|lb|abbr=on}}, and in one of three studies from Oregon be the most numerous prey species (second most numerous in the other two).<ref name="Reynolds" /><ref name= Watson/> This species was also the second most numerous food species in Alberta throughout the year and the most important prey by weight.<ref name= Schaffer/> [[Eastern cottontail]]s (''Sylvilagus floridanus''), also averaging some {{convert|1500|g|lb|abbr=on}} in mass per the study (and thus mostly consisting of adult cottontails in their prime), were the most significant prey both by weight (42.3%) and number (13.3%) in [[Apache-Sitgreaves National Forest]] of [[Arizona]]. Eastern cottontails are also taken regularly in [[New York (state)|New York]] and [[Pennsylvania]].<ref name= Meng/><ref name= Rogers>{{cite journal |last1= Rogers |first1= A. S. |last2= DeStefano |first2= S. |last3= Ingraldi |first3= M. F. |year=2006 |title= Diet, prey delivery rates, and prey biomass of northern goshawks in east-central Arizona |journal= Studies in Avian Biology |volume= 31 |page= 219 }}</ref> In some parts of the range, larger leporids may be attacked, extending to the {{convert|2410|g|lb|abbr=on}} [[Black-tailed jackrabbit|black-tailed]] (''Lepus californicus'') and the {{convert|3200|g|lb|abbr=on}} [[white-tailed jackrabbit]] (''Lepus townsendii'').<ref name=Drennan>{{cite journal |last1= Drennan |first1= J. E. |year=2006 |title= Northern goshawk food habits and goshawk prey species habitats |journal= Studies in Avian Biology |volume= 31 |pages= 198–227 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Lim | first1 = B. K. | year = 1987 | title = Lepus townsendii | journal = Mammalian Species Archive | volume = 288 | pages = 1–6 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Best | first1 = T. L. | year = 1996 | title = Lepus californicus | journal = Mammalian Species Archive | volume = 530 | pages = 1–10 }}</ref><ref>Chapman, J. A., & Flux, J. E. (1990). ''Rabbits, hares and pikas: status survey and conservation action plan''. IUCN.</ref> American goshawks also take [[pika]]s, much smaller cousins of rabbits and hares, but they are at best supplementary prey for American goshawks.<ref name= Watson/>

===Other birds===
[[File:Colaptes auratus FWS.jpg|thumb|upright|[[Woodpecker]]s such as [[northern flicker]]s often fall victim to goshawks]]
Some 21 species of [[woodpecker]] have been reported from northern goshawk food studies around the world. With their relatively slow, undulating flight adult and fledged woodpeckers can easily be overtaken by hunting goshawks, not to mention their habitat preferences frequently put them within active goshawk ranges. Most of the widespread species from North America have been observed as prey, most commonly relatively large woodpeckers such as the {{convert|134|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[northern flicker]] (''Colaptes auratus'') in North America. Indeed, the flicker is the third most regularly reported prey species in America.<ref name= Zawadzka/><ref name= Schreven>Schreven, K. ''Een luchtbukskogeltje in een braakbal van een Havik Accipiter gentilis, in de context van jacht en loodvergifti-ging''. De Takkeling, 225–229.</ref> In south-central [[Wyoming]], the northern flicker was the second most numerous prey species and it was the main prey species in a study from [[New Mexico]] (here making up 26.4% of prey by number).<ref name= Squires2>{{cite journal |last1= Squires |first1= J. R. |year=2000 |title= Food habits of northern goshawks nesting in south central Wyoming |journal= The Wilson Bulletin |volume= 112 |issue= 4|pages= 536–539 |doi=10.1676/0043-5643(2000)112[0536:fhongn]2.0.co;2|s2cid= 85923264 }}</ref><ref>Kennedy, P. L. (1989). ''The nesting ecology of Cooper's hawks and northern goshawks in the Jemez Mountains, NM: a summary of results, 1984-1988 (Final Report)''. Santa Fe National Forest: U.S.D.A. Forest Service.</ref> All sizes of woodpeckers available are taken from the {{convert|25.6|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[downy woodpecker]] (''Picoides pubescens'') to the {{convert|287|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[pileated woodpecker]] (''Dryocopus pileatus'') in North America.<ref name= Zawadzka/><ref name= Schaffer/><ref name= Promessi>Promessi, R. L., Matson, J. O., & Flores, M. (2004). ''Diets of nesting northern goshawks in the Warner Mountains, California''. ''Western North American Naturalist'', 359–363.</ref> In many areas, American goshawks will pursue [[water bird]]s of several varieties, although they rarely form a large portion of the diet. Perhaps the most often recorded water birds in the diet are [[duck]]s. All told, 32 waterfowl have been recorded in Eurasian and American goshawks' diet.<ref name="Kenward" /> The ducks of the genus ''[[Aythya]]'' are somewhat frequently recorded as well, especially since their tree-nesting habits may frequently put them in the hunting range of nesting goshawks.<ref name=Smithers2005>{{cite journal |last1=Smithers |first1=B. L. |last2=Boal |first2=C. W. |last3=Andersen |first3=D. E. |url=http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/jrr/v039n03/p00264-p00273.pdf |title=Northern Goshawk diet in Minnesota: An analysis using video recording systems |journal=Journal of Raptor Research |year=2005 |volume=39 |issue=3 |pages=264–273}}</ref><ref name=Lewis2006/> Similarly, the [[wood duck]] (''Aix sponsa'') may be more vulnerable than most waterfowl at their tree nests.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Bacon |first1=B.R. |year=1983 |title=Goshawk and Red Fox predate Wood Ducks in trap |journal=Passenger Pigeon |volume=45 |page=129}}</ref> Adult [[common eider]]s (''Somateria mollissima''), the largest northern duck at {{convert|2066|g|lb|abbr=on}}, have also been captured by goshawks.<ref name= "CRC"/><ref>{{cite journal |last1=Møller |first1=A. (2010). ''Waders: their breeding, haunts and watchers''. A&C Black.</ref> 

Corvids as aforementioned are quite important prey. Although they take fewer passerines than other northern ''Accipiters'', smaller types of songbirds can still be regionally important to the diet. This is especially true of the [[thrush (bird)|thrush]]es.<ref name= "CRC"/><ref name="Verdal" /><ref name= Schreven/><ref>{{cite journal |last1= Sonerud |first1= G. A. |last2= Steen |first2= R. |last3= Selås |first3= V. |last4= Aanonsen |first4= O. M. |last5= Aasen |first5= G. H. |last6= Fagerland |first6= K. Nonetheless, more than a hundred passerines have been recorded their diet beyond these families. Most widespread passerine families from North America and Europe have occasional losses to goshawks, including [[tyrant flycatcher]]s, [[shrike]]s, [[vireo]]s, [[lark]]s, [[swallow]]s, [[nuthatch]]es, [[treecreeper]]s, [[mimid]]s, [[pipit]]s and [[wagtail]]s, [[starling]]s, [[New World warbler]]s, [[Cardinalidae|cardinalids]], [[icterid]]s, [[finch]]es. ===Interspecies predatory relationships===
[[File:Accipiter gentilis chasing Pandion haliaetus.jpg|thumb|Chasing an [[osprey]], most likely to [[Kleptoparasitism|rob]] it of food, but the osprey is even considered possible prey]]
Eurasian goshawks are often near the top of the avian food chain in forested biomes but face competition for food resources from various other predators. The mean prey mass for [[Sharp-shinned hawk|sharp-shinned]] and [[Cooper's hawk]]s in North America is between about 10 and 30% of their own mass, whereas the mean prey of American goshawks is between about 25 and 50% of their mass and therefore are the goshawks takes prey that is on average relatively much larger.<ref name= Reynolds/><ref name= Storer>{{cite journal |last1= Storer |first1= R. W. |year=1966 |title= Sexual dimorphism and food habits in three North American accipiters |journal= The Auk |volume= 83 |issue= 3|pages= 423–436 |doi=10.2307/4083053|jstor= 4083053 }}</ref> 

In many of the ecosystems that they inhabit, American goshawks compete with resources with other predators, particularly where they take sizeable numbers of lagomorphs. About a dozen mammalian and avian predators in each area primarily consume [[snowshoe hare]]s alongside goshawks in the American boreal forest regions where these became primary staple foods. Like those cohabitant predators, the goshawk suffers declines during the low portion of the lagomorph's breeding cycles, which rise and fall cyclically every 10 years. However, even where these are primary food sources, the American goshawk is less specialized than many other raptors and can alternate their food selection, often taking equal or greater numbers of tree squirrels and woodland birds. Due to this dietary variation, the northern goshawk is less affected than other raptorial birds by prey population cycles and tends to not be depleted by resource competition.<ref name= Doyle>Doyle, F. I. & Smith, J. M. N. (1994). "Population responses of northern goshawks to the 10-year cycle in numbers of snowshoe hares". ''Stud. Avian Biol.'' no. 16:122–129.</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1= Krebs |first1= C. J. |last2= Boutin |first2= S. |last3= Boonstra |first3= R. |last4= Sinclair |first4= A. R. E. |year= 1995 |title= Impact of food and predation on the snowshoe hare cycle |journal= Science |volume= 269 |issue= 5227|pages= 1112–5 |doi=10.1126/science.269.5227.1112|pmid= 17755536 |bibcode= 1995Sci...269.1112K |hdl= 1807/488 |s2cid= 17661939 |hdl-access= free }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1= Hik |first1= D. S. |year= 1995 |title= Does risk of predation influence population dynamics? Evidence from cyclic decline of snowshoe hares |journal= Wildlife Research |volume= 22 |issue= 1|pages= 115–129 |doi=10.1071/wr9950115|doi-access= free }}</ref>

Despite their propensity to pursue relatively large prey and capability to pursue alternate prey, American goshawks can be locally outcompeted for resources by species that are more adaptable and flexible, especially in terms of habitat and prey. Most northern buteonine hawks largely take small rodents such as [[vole]]s (which are usually ignored by goshawks) but can adapt to nearly any other type of prey when the staple local rodent prey populations go down.<ref name= Marti>Marti, C. D., Korpimäki, E., & Jaksić, F. M. (1993). "Trophic structure of raptor communities: a three-continent comparison and synthesis". ''Current Ornithology'', pp. 47–137. Springer US.</ref> Comparisons with goshawks and [[red-tailed hawk]] nesting in abutting areas of [[Arizona]] (other large common ''Buteos'' like [[Swainson's hawk]]s (''Buteo swainsonii'') and [[ferruginous hawk]]s (''Buteo regalis'') utilize open habitats and so do not come into conflict with goshawks) shows the red-tailed hawks as being able to take a broader range of prey than goshawks and nest in more varied habitats, the latter species being perhaps the most commonly seen, widespread and adaptable of diurnal American raptors.<ref>{{cite journal | last1 = Gatto | first1 = A. E. | last2 = Grubb | first2 = T. G. | last3 = Chambers | first3 = C. L. | year = 2006 | title = Red-tailed hawk dietary overlap with northern goshawks on the Kaibab Plateau, Arizona | journal = J. Raptor Res. | volume = 39 | pages = 439–444 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1= La Sorte |first1= F. A. |last2= Mannan |first2= R. W. |last3= Reynolds |first3= R. T. |last4= Grubb |first4= T. G. |year= 2004 |title= Habitat associations of sympatric red-tailed hawks and northern goshawks on the Kaibab Plateau |journal= Journal of Wildlife Management |volume= 68 |issue= 2|pages= 307–317 |doi=10.2193/0022-541x(2004)068[0307:haosrh]2.0.co;2|s2cid= 85897115 }}</ref><ref>Preston, C. R. (2000). ''Red-tailed hawk''. Stackpole Books.</ref> 

American goshawks are less prone to nesting outside of mature forests and take larger numbers of mammals as opposed to abundant birds than in Europe. This may be in part due to heavier competition from a greater diversity of raptors in North America. In [[Europe]], the goshawk only co-exists with the much smaller sparrowhawk within its own genus, while in North America, it lives with the intermediately-sized [[Cooper's hawk]]. The latter species much more readily nests in semi-open and developed areas of North America than goshawks there and hunts a broad assemblage of medium-sized birds, whereas such prey is more readily available to male goshawks from Europe than to goshawks in North America. Although the Cooper's hawk usually avoids and loses individual contests against the larger goshawk, its adaptability has allowed it to become the most widespread and commonly found North American ''Accipiter''.<ref name= Kenward/><ref name= Deforestation>Kenward, R. E. (1996). ''Goshawk Adaptation to Deforestation: Does Europe Differ From North. Raptors in Human Landscapes: Adaptation to Built and Cultivated Environments'', 233.</ref><ref name= Wight>Reynolds, R. T. & Wight, H. M. (1978). "Distribution, density, and productivity of accipiter hawks breeding in Oregon". ''The Wilson Bulletin'', 182–196.</ref>

American goshawks are slightly smaller on average than their European counterparts and can be up to 10% smaller in mass than red-tailed hawks. However, studies have indicated that the goshawk has, beyond its superior speed and agility, has stronger feet and a more forceful attack than that of the red-tailed hawk. All in all, individual competitions between red-tailed hawks and goshawks can go either way and neither is strongly likely to deter the other from nesting given their distinct nesting habitats.<ref name= Kenward/><ref>Goslow, G. E. (1971). "The attack and strike of some North American raptors". ''The Auk'', 815-827.</ref><ref>Sprunt, A., & May, J. B. (1955). ''North American birds of prey''. Published under the sponsorship of the National Audubon Society by Harper.</ref> Other raptors, including most medium to large-sized owls as well as red-tailed hawks and falcons, will use nests built by northern goshawks, even when goshawks are still in the area.<ref name="Territory occupancy and habitat pat">{{cite journal |last1= Woodbridge |first1= B. |last2= Detrich |first2= P. J. |year= 1994 |title= Territory occupancy and habitat patch size of northern goshawks in the southern Cascades of California |journal= Studies in Avian Biology |volume= 16 |pages= 83–87 }}</ref>

To many other raptorial birds, the northern goshawk is more significant as a predatory threat than as competition. American goshawk predate other accipiters such as the {{convert|136|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[sharp-shinned hawk]] and the {{convert|440|g|lb|abbr=on}} [[Cooper's hawk]].<ref>Becker, T. E.; Smith, D. G.; Bosakowski, T. (2006). "Habitat, food habits, and productivity of northern goshawks nesting in Connecticut". Studies in Avian Biology. 31: 119–125.</ref><ref name=Lewis2006>{{cite journal |last1=Lewis |first1=Stephen B. |last2=Titus |first2=Kimberly |last3=Fuller |first3=Mark R. |url=http://fresc.usgs.gov/products%5Cpapers%5C1547_Fuller.pdf |title=Northern Goshawk Diet During the Nesting Season in Southeast Alaska |doi=10.2193/0022-541X(2006)70[1151:NGDDTN]2.0.CO;2 |year=2006 |journal=Journal of Wildlife Management |volume=70 |issue=4 |pages=1151–1160|s2cid=59493648 }}</ref> Among Buteo hawks, the adults of {{convert|424|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[broad-winged hawk]] (''Buteo platypterus''), {{convert|610|g|lb|abbr=on}} [[red-shouldered hawk]] (''Buteo lineatus'') and the {{convert|1065|g|lb|abbr=on}} [[red-tailed hawk]] are known to be killed.<ref>Smithers, B. L.; Boal, C. W.; Andersen, D. E. (2005). "Northern Goshawk diet in Minnesota: An analysis using video recording systems" (PDF). Journal of Raptor Research. 39 (3): 264–273.</ref><ref name= Arizona>Reynolds, R. T., Joy, S. M. & Leslie, D. G. (1994). "Nest productivity, fidelity, and spacing of northern goshawks in northern Arizona". ''Stud. Avian Biol.'' no. 16:106–113.</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1= Crannell |first1= D. |last2= DeStefano |first2= S. |year= 1992 |title= An aggressive interaction between a northern goshawk and a red-tailed hawk |journal= Journal of Raptor Research |volume= 26 |issue= 4|pages= 269–270 }}</ref> American goshawks also pose a threat to [[osprey]] (''Pandion haliaetus'') chicks.

==Status==

The breeding range of the American goshawk extends over one-third of North America, a total area of over {{convert|30,000,000|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}} for Eurasian and American goshawk. There are a broadly estimated 150,000–300,000 individuals in North America. most western populations at mid-latitudes have approximately 3.6–10.7 pairs/{{convert|100|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}}. A total of 107 nesting territories (1991–1996) were located on a {{convert|1754|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}} study area on the Kaibab Plateau, AZ, resulting in a density of 8.4 pairs/{{convert|100|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}}. The estimated density in Pennsylvania (1.17 pairs/{{convert|100|km2|sqmi|abbr=on}}) suggests that eastern populations may occur at lower densities than western populations, but densities of eastern populations may increase as these populations recover. Typically, populations at far northern latitudes may occur at lower densities than those of southwestern and western populations in North America.<ref name=Ferguson-Lees/><ref name="Cornell" /><ref>Kimmel, J. T. and R. H. Yahner (1994). ''The northern goshawk in Pennsylvania: habitat use, survey protocols, and status (Final Report)''. Pennsylvania State Univ., University Park: School For. Resour.</ref> Although median densities was similar, populations are overall much denser in Europe than in North America. Per the [[IUCN]], the global population of American and [[Eurasian goshawk]] is estimated to consist of 1 million to nearly 2.5 million birds, making this one of the most numerous species complex in its diverse family (the [[red-tailed hawk]] and [[black kite]] both may have a similar global population size, whereas the [[Eurasian sparrowhawk]] and [[common buzzard]] are possibly slightly more numerous than goshawks despite their smaller ranges).<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" /><ref>{{cite iucn |author=BirdLife International |date=2016 |title=''Accipiter nisus'' |volume=2016 |article-number=e.T22695624A93519953 |doi=10.2305/IUCN.UK.2016-3.RLTS.T22695624A93519953.en |access-date=12 November 2021}}</ref><ref>{{cite iucn |author=BirdLife International |date=2020 |title=''Buteo buteo'' |volume=2020 |article-number=e.T61695117A181770099 |doi=10.2305/IUCN.UK.2020-3.RLTS.T61695117A181770099.en |access-date=12 November 2021}}</ref><ref>Sauer, J., Niven, D., Hines, J., Ziolkowski Jr, D., Pardieck, K. L., Fallon, J. E., & Link, W. (2017). ''The North American breeding bird survey, results and analysis 1966-2015''. USGS Patuxent Wildlife Research Center, Laurel, MD, USA.</ref>

Based on studies from [[Gotland]], Finland and the [[southwestern United States]], annual mortality for adults is 15–21%, however, feather results indicate that annual mortality for adult Eurasian goshawks is up to 7% higher in Europe than American goshawks in North America.<ref name="Cornell" /><ref name="Kenward" /><ref name= Hoglund>Hoglund, N. H. 1964. "The hawk Accipiter gentilis Linne in Fennos Kandia" (English translation). ''Viltrevy'' no. 2:195–269.</ref><ref name= Bijlsma>Bijlsma, R. G. (1993). ''Ecologische atlas van de Nederlandse roofvogels''. Haarlem, Schuyt & Co.</ref><ref>Haukioja, E. & Haukioja, H. (1970). "Mortality rates of Finnish and Swedish goshawks (Accipiter gentilis)". ''Finnish Game Research'', no. 31:13–20.</ref> In many parts of the range, historic populations decreased regionally due to human persecution (especially shooting), disturbance and epidemic loss of habitat, especially during the 19th century and early 20th.<ref name=Ferguson-Lees/> Some states, like Pennsylvania, paid $5 bounties on Goshawks in the 1930s.<ref name="Cornell" />

In the 1950s–1960s declines were increasingly linked with [[pesticide pollution]]. However, in early 1970s pesticide levels in the United States for goshawks were low. Eggshell thinning has not been a problem for most populations, although [[California]] eggshells (weight and thickness index) pre-1947 (pre-DDT) to 1947—1964 (DDT in use) declined some 8-12%. In [[Illinois]], migratory goshawks during the winter of 1972-1973 invasion year contained less organochlorine and PCB residues than did other raptors, however, these birds were probably from nonagricultural, northern forests.<ref>{{cite journal |last1= Snyder |first1= N. F. R. |last2= Snyder |first2= H. A. |last3= Lincer |first3= J. A. |last4= Reynolds |first4= R. T. |year= 1973 |title= Organochlorines, heavy metals, and the biology of North American accipiters |journal= BioScience |volume= 23 |issue= 5|pages= 300–305 |doi=10.2307/1296439|jstor= 1296439 }}</ref><ref>Anderson, D. W. and J. J. Hickey. 1972. "Eggshell changes in certain North American birds". In ''Proceedings of the XVth International Ornitholological Congress'', edited by H. H. Voous, 514-540. Leiden: P. J. Brill.</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1= Havera |first1= S. P. |last2= Duzan |first2= R. E. |year= 1986 |title= Organochlorine and PCB residues in tissues of raptors from Illinois 1966-1981 |journal= Bull. Environ. Contam. Toxicol |volume= 36 |issue= 1|pages= 23–32 |doi=10.1007/bf01623470|pmid= 3079646 |s2cid= 28539093 }}</ref> 

Seemingly the remaining persistent conservation threat to goshawks, given their seeming overall resilience (at the species level) to both persecution and pesticides, is deforestation. Timber harvests are known to destroy many nests and adversely regional populations.<ref name= Deforestation>Kenward, R. E. (1996). ''Goshawk Adaptation to Deforestation: Does Europe Differ From North. Raptors in Human Landscapes: Adaptation to Built and Cultivated Environments'', 233.</ref><ref>Reynolds, R. T. (1989). ''Accipiters''. Paper read at Proceedings of the Western Raptor Management Symposium and Workshop.</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1= Crocker-Bedford |first1= D. C. |year= 1990 |title= Goshawk reproduction and forest management |journal= Wildl. Soc. Bull. |volume= 18 |pages= 262–269 }}</ref> Harvest methods that create extensive areas of reduced forest canopy cover, dropping to cover less than 35-40%, may be especially detrimental as cases of this usually cause all goshawks to disappear from the area.<ref name="Bright-Smith" /><ref>{{cite journal |last1= Beier |first1= P. |last2= Drennan |first2= J. E. |year= 1997 |title= Forest structure and prey abundance in foraging areas of northern goshawks |journal= Ecological Applications |volume= 7 |issue= 2|pages= 564–571 |doi=10.2307/2269521|jstor= 2269521 }}</ref> However, the mortality rates due to foresting practices are unknown and it is possible that some mature goshawks may simply be able to shift to other regions when a habitat becomes unsuitable but this is presumably unsustainable in the long-term.<ref name="Cornell" /> In harvest forests of [[California]], where overstory trees are frequently removed, goshawks have been found to successfully remain as breeding species as long as some mature stands are left intact. Despite the decline of habitat quality and the frequent disturbances, this region's goshawks breeding success rates somewhat improbably did not reduce.<ref>{{cite journal |last1= Hargis |first1= C. D. |last2= McCarthy |first2= C. |last3= Perloff |first3= R. D. |year= 1994 |title= Home ranges and habitats of northern goshawks in eastern California |journal= Stud. Avian Biol. |volume= 16 |pages= 66–74 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1= Woodbridge |first1= B. |last2= Detrich |first2= P. J. |year= 1994 |title= Territory occupancy and habitat patch size of northern goshawks in the southern Cascades of California |journal= Stud. Avian Biol. |volume= 16 |pages= 83–87 }}</ref> Based on habitat usage studied in [[New Jersey]] and [[New York (state)|New York]], this adaptability is not seen everywhere, as here nests were further from human habitation than expected on the basis of available habitat, an observation suggesting that disturbance regionally can reduce habitat quality.<ref>Speiser, R., & Bosakowski, T. (1987). "Nest site selection by northern goshawks in northern New Jersey and southeastern New York". ''Condor'', 387-394.</ref> Similarly, studies from the [[American southwest]] and [[Canada]] have indicated that heavily logged areas caused strong long-term regional declines for goshawks. In [[Arizona]], it was found that even when the nests were left intact, the noisy timber harvest work often caused failure of nesting during the incubation stage, and all nesting attempts that were occurring within {{convert|50|to|100|m|ft|abbr=on}} of active logging failed, frequently after parents abandoned the nest.<ref name= Boal2>{{cite journal |last1= Boal |first1= C. W. |year= 1994 |title= A photographic and behavioral guide to aging nestling Northern Goshawks |journal= Studies in Avian Biology |volume= 16 |pages= 32–40 }}</ref><ref>Reynolds, R. T., Graham, R. T., & Reiser, M. H. (1992). ''Management recommendations for the northern goshawk in the southwestern United States''.</ref><ref>{{cite journal |last1= Doyle |first1= F. I. |year= 2006 |title= Goshawks in Canada: population responses to harvesting and the appropriateness of using standard bird monitoring techniques to assess their status |journal= Studies in Avian Biology |volume= 31 |page= 135 }}</ref> Other noisy activity, such as camping, have also caused nests to fail.<ref name="Speiser2">{{cite journal |last1= Speiser |first1= R. |year= 1992 |title= Notes on the natural history of the northern goshawk |journal= Kingbird |volume= 42 |pages= 133–137 }}</ref> Wildlife researchers and biologists do not seem to negatively affect goshawk nests, as they know to keep forays to the nest brief and capture of adult goshawks for radio-tagging was found to not harm their success at raising broods.<ref name="Speiser2" />

In North America, several non-governmental conservation organizations petitioned the Department of Interior, [[United States Fish & Wildlife Service]] (1991 & 1997) to list the goshawk as "threatened" or "endangered" under the authority of the [[Endangered Species Act]]. Both petitions argued for listing primarily on the basis of historic and ongoing nesting habitat loss, specifically the loss of old-growth and mature forest stands throughout the goshawk's known range. In both instances, the U.S. Fish & Wildlife Service concluded that listing was not warranted, but state and federal natural resource agencies responded during the petition process with standardized and long-term goshawk inventory and monitoring efforts, especially throughout U.S. Forest Service lands in the Western U.S. The [[United States Forest Service]] (US Dept of Agriculture) has listed the goshawk as a "sensitive species", while it also benefits from various protections at the state level. In North America, the goshawk is federally protected under the [[Migratory Bird Treaty Act of 1918]] by an amendment incorporating native birds of prey into the Act in 1972. The American goshawk (as subspecies of northern goshawk, currently [[Eurasian goshawk]]) is also listed in Appendix II of the Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species ([[CITES]]).<ref name="Woodbridge2006" />
